Representative_Docs: ['i mean it entirely depends on what you d consider a good career from an employability and financial perspective it generally is quite good there are lots of jobs out there in programming but the pay is normally strongly based upon your skill level i started at after dropping out of college and landing an apprenticeship and by the end of that the company kept me on as a junior php developer at around k uk based i ve been doing it for about years at this point i now earn around k as a lead devops engineer i have spent alot of personal time learning new tools and methodologies which i think is what allowed me to move up the ladder quite quickly it can take alot longer to start earning good money depending on how much effort you wanna put into it i just kinda fell into web development but there are so many different variations of programming you could get into which are all slightly different there s application development i e desktop apps mobile development web development machine learning data science sysadmin networking depending which one of these interests you you would pick one of a few certain languages to start with this is only a few of the different areas i mostly work with web and mobile app development teams so this is a rough idea of where you could start with those for web development if you like the aesthetic side of websites the actual look feel and user experience you most likely would be best starting with html css and javascript this is frontend web development if like me you prefer just solving problems rather than worrying about aesthetics you d wanna look into some server side language this is backend web development and there are alot of options just for this but python php and ruby are often some of the easiest to get started with for mobile app development i d say java would be a good place to start which will allow you to develop android apps java can also be used for web dev and many other things and is normally taught at universities from what i hear if you wanna develop iphone apps then you wanna look into swift although it s apparently a bit trickier to use there is also cross platform to consider but i don t have any experience with this i just know its out there you ll also find that the company you work for can have a huge bearing on how much you enjoy the job some companies have really good cultures and you can feel really relaxed while going to work every day while others will happily let you destroy your soul to meet a deadline biggest piece of advice i d give you it is to take a bit time to read up on the different areas and what kinds of work you d be doing its not always that important where you start because alot of the skills of any language are quite transferrable i started with php but nowadays i only really use golang for application development and spend alot of my time scripting various processes using tools like terraform docker and kubernetes if you ve never done any programming before i really think you need to get started with some basic tutorials potentially try a few different languages just so you can see whether you enjoy it programming is something you ll need to enjoy if your gonna do it everyday otherwise it s kind of analogous to learning calculus if you hate maths you don t really learn it very well if you don t find some level of enjoyment in it i d recommend going on udemy com to see what courses are available in the development section if you don t wanna pay for them understandably you can always use udemy as a reference for what is out there and then have a look for similar things on youtube but normally the quality isn t as good once you have an idea of what you want to learn though just google it seriously there are so many blog posts and tutorials out there you can normally find a half decent one quite quickly i know there s alot of crap here bit honestly i don t think there can be a short answer to this hopefully this helps in some way', 'dude holy shit thanks for these feedbacks i m actually learning stuff and i m super grateful first i must say that being overly optimistic in year as indicated on that chart is so me right now i m taking two classes and heavily focused on research life is good i go to the gym make fancy meals take some time off on the weekends to hang out etc but i must also admit that i question my decision for coming to grad school every time i m stuck doing homework and knowing my friends with a b s are making more money with time off on weekends right before graduate school i seriously looked for jobs but my options were pretty much to either do plain boring coding or be an engineer in like designing and testing stuff in fact i talked to companies such as quanlab microsoft google wolfram alpha when they visited my school and all said that having a phd is really needed if i want my job to provide me with ample research opportunities moreover when i enter the workforce i d like to be in control of my time and projects this means i need to be at a managerial level and those are almost always people with phds the other honest truth is that i felt i was incredibly unequipped to be useful to any company beyond doing menial mechanical tasks i hoped going through grad school will provide me with enough critical thinking skills that i can be actually useful in r amp d since that s what i like to do will i master out definitely yes if the stars have aligned by then i have no shame of dropping out and i will do so at any point as long as i have been able to secure a job that i see myself happy at besides research assistanceships i have not had jobs elsewhere so i haven t tasted big money but i can t wait hahah lastly you make a great point why stay in physics when it s clear my interests are in cs ml type stuff apparently i clearly give off this vibe so much that my undergraduate advisor asked me the same question my reason was that i am deeply interested in the mathematical treatment of physical phenomena think about the formalism of qm or the incredibly versatile concepts of statistical physics or probability theory even statistics you see this is what excites me pure math is boring but i found physics to incorporate just the right amount of applications alongside theoretical treatment so why not major in cs because i didn t like the course track and i did try it the cs b s track had too many courses relevant for someone who planned on becoming a software developer as it should have i simply wasn t interested in courses like operating systems discrete math and ethics of coding only two courses out of the full course load interested me algorithms and data structures again the common denominator here is that these two courses get down to the bottom line of how data is dealt with on a computer and how we can be smart and creative to come up with clever algorithms machine learning although a cs field shares many of my interests it s theoretical and applicable and it is intellectually stimulating and offers much room for research that involves mathematical modeling and thinking cleverly so this is why i picked physics and now i like to bridge the gap to ml in terms of projects i m doing my best to pick up ml related topics i currently have this image recognition project and i have an offer from a national lab to work on ml algorithms for studying spin glass transitions question my ultimate goal is to be an ml research analyst in the industry and i ve been looking at those job postings and luckily they list physics phd as a qualification but it can t just be that simple because many physicists get their degrees without ever really coding so in order to make myself really qualified as a physicist what skills should i pick up along the way we had a google rep talk to us and i asked him how the hiring goes when a physicist is competing with a professionally trained data scientist for a data science job posting and he said they know the former has shortcomings but it really comes down to is it clear that you re someone who can learn new topics fast and are you workable is it true my biggest fear is that i m never good enough to compete with someone who is professionally trained in say ml or data science both fields of my interest to my future career', 'i occasionally like to read r csmajors and r cscareerquestions and i ve noticed a fair bit of interest in the more academic topics in cs ml ai most commonly and also things like security theory and more there s not a lot good advice on how to actually get started in those though while there are a number of potential ways to gain real experience with those topics one of the best ways is to start early as a university student and get involved in undergraduate research who are you and what do you know about research without giving too many details i m a researcher in a cs department at a major university i m not a professor rather i m someone who s a soft money academic whose responsibilities are mainly to conduct research and keep the money flowing in this includes occasionally bringing in undergrads to help me with various tasks why should i care about doing research as an undergrad undergrad research is a great opportunity available to university students but from my experience few actually take advantage of it it s surprising because it has a number of nice benefits you get to apply the skills you learn in the classroom you can get paid get course credit or both you re not bound to a strict schedule so this job respects the fact that you re a student you get experience working on a non trivial project you have the opportunity to finish something you can show off like a poster a lightning talk or a workshop or conference paper you learn how to be peers with people much more experienced than you you gain lots of soft skills with all the collaboration and public speaking expected in research you start building up a professional network with some really well connected people little known fact academics tend to know a lot of people in industry government and other sectors beyond just the university if produce presentable resarch you get to travel to conferences and workshops if grad school and especially a phd program is an possibility in your future research experience is what sets competitive applicants above the rest what do undergraduate resarchers do it depends on the supervisor and the project as well as individual students interests and experience levels in my work i like to ask new undergraduate researchers to write code to pull metrics from the data i ve collected as well as improve and expand my data collection systems this is valuable to me because it frees me to focus on the analysis i want to publish and it s valuable for the students because these small well defined tasks give them a practical introduction to a real research agenda and methods as students gain confidence i have them come up with interesting questions of their own and ways to expand the data in order to answer those questions my end goal is to put students in a position where they can investigate those questions and compile presentable results at the very least that means something they can put on a poster and discuss confidently with other researchers at a poster session at a conference for the most ambitious students and especially those with phd aspirations i guide them to refine and expand their research questions so we can write up a proper conference paper with students taking the lead what are the expectations for undergraduate researchers again it depends on the supervisor and project for me because i don t take on many undergraduate researchers two at any given time at most i invest a lot of time and mentorship into the few students i do work with in return i expect them to be reliable and commit to working with me for at least two semesters consecutively new undergraduate researchers require a lot of guidance and orientation when they first start so it tends to take a while before they can produce presentable work i also expect students to ask lots of questions because being honest about what you don t know is a key skill in research how do i get started talk to your professors even if they don t have active research agendas as could be the case with faculty at non doctoral granting institutions or projects directly related to your interests odds are good that they can refer you to someone else who s looking for part time help some cs departments like the one where i earned my bachelor s degree might also announce research opportunities by email but those generally boil down to talk to prof so and so about this opportunity when you meet with a potential research supervisor relax being on the other side of the table i m not out to grill you or make you do pointless whiteboarding exercises i m just interested in seeing if my projects sound neat to you and figuring out how much time you can commit working on things in my experience i ve had the best outcomes with students who start at the end of their second year i m able to get them up to speed more quickly and thoroughly when they work with me full time during that summer and they have plenty of time to finish producing presentable work in time to show off for job and grad school applications how do i get the most out of it speak up and tell your supervisor what you want don t be passive and just take orders if you want to be on a paper say so if you have a particular angle for a study you want to explore bring it up for discussion if you read something cool that you want to incorporate into the work share it if you d prefer to work with a different team or topic that s fine what are the downsides long story short your mileage may vary there are some research groups that churn through undergrads as if they re a disposable resource there are supervisors who often understandably have too much on their hands and can t dedicate to mentoring students as i can as a pure researcher there are groups that don t have much internal cohesion or esprit de corps to help one another out basically a research group is a workplace and workplaces have their own unique cultures you probably won t learn the latest frameworks best practices and hottest development tools doing research research code is indeed often ugly hacky and unmaintainable i m a rare bird in my research community in that i have considerable industry experience too so i m able to mitigate this to some degree even then though maintainable software isn t as big of a priority in research as present interesting results if you re on a path to writing a conference paper you ll ideally find yourself with a lot of responsibility as the lead author this means reconciling the often conflicting opinions of your co authors who are almost certainly far more experienced than you this is a natural part of the internal tension in research but it can be overwhelming at first for students trying to figure out whether to listen to prof a more than dr b or vice versa bonus how do i get involved in this when i m no longer a student it s not impossible but it s much more difficult i ll start with the bad news you have a lot of things going against you when you re no longer a student because as mentioned earlier getting started in research requires a lot of guidance and mentorship at the beginning it s really not something you can learn on your own i ve had the best results with students who work in the same room as me full time for a summer if you re out of school it s likely the best you can do is part time and mostly remotely neither of which is ideal research is done in a community and it s hard to be taken seriously in a community if you don t commit to it and if you don t have someone vouching for you that said there some things that can improve your chances and make your experience more productive work somewhere that employs phds as researchers reach out to them they might know of opportunities you can get involved in and you re likelier to receive a response and have a face to face chat if you re in the same organization likewise if you have friends who are researchers or are in phd programs ask them as well become familiar with the academic literature in the topic you re interested in the best way to do this is to look at the syllabus for graduate level courses to see what papers they read and in which venues those papers were published bonus what are things i should look for in a research supervisor group my personal preference is that aspiring undergrad researchers most need proper mentorship and guidance that means either your supervisor or someone in his her research group will take on the responsibility of helping you get settled into the rhythm of research that should be someone who s approachable and happy to answer any question you have without that initial guidance it s really easy for students to waste time instead of working towards being productive researchers who know what they re doing if i were a student i would ask questions like what are the relevant papers i should read what level of supervision should i expect how often are we going to have face to face status meetings how often are we going to have in person work sessions what deadline are we working toward where did your other undergraduate research assistants end up what else ask me anything']

Name: 1_python_language_languages_java
Representative_Docs: ['but at the end of the day most python jobs are not making django flask apps python is used for data analysis ai ml devops etc python is a go to language for linux scripting nowadays in fact it s included by default in most distributions which you kinda do care about as your application will eventually have to land on some kind of a server or maybe you are building a web application that recommends movies and want to give users accurate suggestions on what to watch so you use that ml this is a real task actually spotify or netflix both do it in that case it makes a lot of sense to build the whole thing in python it will cut down development time and maintenance costs compared to running your machine learning system in python and web application interacting with it written in javascript similarly you can make web apps in java c using spring boot or asp net core it s possible but the ecosystem is associated with large scale enterprises that may not necessarily be sexy i am scared of sexy development i want a stable ecosystem that behaves in a predictable way not a hey imma blow up in your face randomly every month last time i have heard of sexy languages it was ruby on rails hype around which only really became stable in release and people using it before that threw away proven tools bankrupted enough companies as is funnily enough these same people moved onto nodejs when it was at it s worst and immature status afterwards i can bet that in few years something else will become hyped and considered super popular what only matters is how polished a tool is and what kind of community is behind it and if it has presence in your country so you can actually find employers and employees net mvc does have a huge backer in microsoft it also actually absorbed a lot of good things from other frameworks for instance you can build state of an art applications in any half decent framework think about it like this our time is limited we have limited time every single day we can t possibly learn everything which is why you eventually specialize in bigger companies yes but it s not because of languages i can pick up a new one in weeks if i have to and i am not boasting any dev past a certain level can pick up languages like candy as long as they are same paradighm to what they already know now however if i decided to pick up game development i would need to learn it essentially from zero and would take a lot of effort despite me knowing languages used for it it s language agnostic skills that take real effort to learn and to keep up with them wouldn t full stack js fit the bill nothing else it would but so would python with flask js or go js personally i would care far more about what kind of project i am building then what tools i am using it s essentially a difference between a pen or a pencil for drawing a forest it s not the tool that you have to learn and that will be a real challenge you will see for yourself soon enough however as even if you go full js route you will see how react front end framework has unique syntax and in fact many programmers use typescript which compiles to javascript and solves problems that have nothing even remotely in common to what you will be doing on the back end side be it via meteor expressjs or sailsjs language might be the same but you know you use english for both a debate Representative_Docs: ['sorry but i don t know what to say to that every data scientist in my team at least has a masters in stats machine learning or something in between all my data scientist friends data according to the u s bureau of labor statistics the reports states that the rise of data science needs will create million job openings by not only the demand will be huge but there will be a dearth in data science talent let us look at the promising data science careers today data scientists data engineer data architect business intelligence developer machine learning engineer i am fairly sure that we all know that with technology changing at a dynamic speed the data science field has been accelerating proliferating and evolving at an equal pace however to break into these lucrative and sought after job roles one must upgrade themselves with the latest technologies', 'uh sounds like i need to communicate some stuff better data analyst and data engineer are not neccessarily senior roles data scientists almost always are data analyst it might be helpful to think of a data analyst as someone who reports to a data scientist they are the lowest on the totem pole in the data science field as far as compensation goes and the title itself have a wide wide range of potential responsibilities ranging from being essentially being a mis named business analyst to being a step below a data scientist in all but name this is where i recommend starting out if you want to break into ds data engineer a data engineer helps set up the pipelines and tools that a data analyst and data scientist use also their roles run the gamut from just being a dba to being almost a data scientist as well technically speaking data engineers do not have the same skillset that is neccessary to become a data scientist though i mentioned that this is a flaw in the industry that you get all these data scientists but they can t do any of the technical work themselves so the work ends up being look similar to a senior or lead data analyst to be honest i don t know precisely what the role entials because i myself am still in my first couple of years in the field but my suspiscion is that the role is essentially becoming synonymous with machine learning engineer all of the data scientists that i reported to were working on upgrading our algorithm from a statistically based on to a ml algorithm gt thank you for the in depth response i have made efforts to transfer internally but the road map for transferring is looking like it ll take at least a year which feels like forever do way you ll always still have work you haven t tasted the despair of applying to every single data analyst and data engineer position on glassdoor for months on end by increasingly shadier companies Representative_Docs: ['when in doubt do tic tac toe you can add features which cover just about every data structure and pattern out there past the base game do have the user define x for an x by x board meaning support anything from x to x boards dynamically based on user input have the user define how many in a row you need to win checking for in a row on a x is very different than checking for in a row on x create a ui graphics instead of printing text out to display the board or make it web based make it player or x players have the players choose what symbol letter to use make a computer player that can play the game make easy average hard versions of the ai ruin all your previous logic by supporting non perfect square boards x or x boards have a database of users and keep their win loss records make an elo system for them write unit tests for your program support network games non hotseat what these will teach you these will push you towards single responsibility methods and separation of concerns relying on input parameters rather than global values should stop most of your hardcoding hacks many of these will cause you to do large refactoring if not entire rewrites if you don t plan ahead for them after you are forced to rewrite for the rd time you will learn why requirements are valuable to know ahead of time basics of ui implementation varies a lot based on which ui library you pick various algorithms and the basic of ai tic tac toe is a relatively simple algorithm in a normal x board you can brute force it and not worry too much about the math you will get introduced into culling branches and mini max once you go into larger boards or force timing constraints easy vs hard on the ai decision time database skills learning how to persist data after program shuts down how to load data on startup unit tests really really enforce not using globals and having each method do only the small piece it is required to do forces you to think about potential non happy paths knowing how to write unit testable code is a big plus in a professional environment that kind of code tends to be very easy to read and maintain networking you can go as far as you want in this anywhere from basic lan to hosting a server for leaderboards you can go overboard and make a minecraft like setup where you need both a client and server to play the game this Name: 4_masters_master_ms_degree
Representative_Docs: ['i think this depends on what your goals are if your goal is to have a career in industry probably getting just a bs in a school with a strong cs program will be sufficient at least within the us the norms could be different in other places in the world not sure you should also make sure to do the typical mix of side projects extracurricular clubs internships and or undergraduate research to help supplement your classes beef up your resume getting an education is only half the reason to go to college getting access to these extracurricular opportunities is the other major reason so i wouldn t pick one school over the other just because one has a combined bs ms degree and the other doesn t pick the one with the more challenging robust curriculum robust extracurriculars bigger career fairs if your goal is to have an specialized career in industry e g you want to work on specifically machine learning or compilers or robotics you should pick extracurriculars that help you learn as much as possible about your domain of interest here getting additional education whether that s a combined bs ms ms or phd may potentially be useful but only if it ll let you gain further experience in your chosen specialization if your goal is to have a career doing research usually in academia but sometimes in industrial research labs like microsoft research you should probably skip doing a master s altogether instead prioritize joining a school that has a strong undergrad research program get involved in research as soon as possible try and perhaps publish a paper or two and try and jump directly to getting into a phd program people who want to take this route will generally do a masters or combined bs ms only if they need more time to develop a strong research portfolio or discovered research late and want more time to explore if this career path is right for them and of course there might be other reasons too personally i decided to do a combined bs ms degree because tuition was free while i was a masters student and this gave me an opportunity to try redesigning and teaching a course from scratch which i thought could be fun but if you don t have a concrete reason for why you want a masters combined or not i don t think it s worth it you re probably better off just graduating on time and gaining on the job experience sooner rather then later i also agree with u reddilada s advice it s always nice to keep your options open the prereqs needed to get into these sorts of programs are generally things that are educational and look good on your resume anyways', 'gt will merely having a bachelors in any way impede my career growth i think it s unlikely it s pretty rare that i see a job description mention a masters degree anywhere but i have seen then always a nice to have not a strict requirement i went back to school while working to get a masters in cs in the hopes that it would make me a better developer i don t think it did that at all i learned a lot of stuff but mostly stuff i never use in my day to day i did quite well academically and i m confident that i wasn t under performing or failing to learn the expected material worth it is subjective but for me i d say if you get a masters with a strong emphasis in something and are able to transition to working with that full time it s worth it if you get a masters where you learned a lot about ai and are able to go from making websites to full time ai developer it s probably a good investment if you want to teach a few classes at a college though with bootcamps becoming as popular as they are i don t even know how true this will be in the future having a masters can make a big difference in your ability to get hired i went through the interview process once and was told i was a solid candidate but that the lack of a masters degree was why i wasn t selected i think they were being honest and they did invite me to reapply if your company is paying for it and you are going in person and you generally enjoyed the academic side of college i think it might be worth it you ll get to know some professors and a bunch of other students some will be traditional students who are going to look up to you a bit and who will be looking for jobs in years others will be older students who might have a lot of business type connections already it never hurts to know people i ended up getting a referral bonus by passing along the resume of a guy i met in that masters program probably unlikely but i ended up moving to the eu and getting a job i was told by the lady who handles the work permit request forms that having an advanced degree would make it much easier i don t know how true that was or how many countries would care but that s probably the only time anyone seemed impressed or happy that i had a masters degree amp x b but i wouldn t do it if you don t think you ll enjoy it i really think whatever career benefit is pretty minor and it takes a lot of time and effort if you kind of enjoy it a bit it s not too bad if you are just doing it to do it it s pretty miserable you want to be better at your current job i really thought i d finish the program and go from like a mid tier developer to a top tier awesome developer at least for me that didn t happen i took classes and basically none of them directly related to my job spending even half the time that i spent on one of those classes and learning about a library or tool or language that my team used would have been more beneficial i hate to say that but i swear it was true at least for me or hell even if i d just spent a fraction of the hours i spent working on my masters degree and just worked longer hours and gotten more done at work i would have stood out as a harder working more dedicated employee and done better at my job amp x b it s all anecdotal anyway but let me finish with this my current job on my current team i m working with a guy who dropped out of high school he was home schooled until high school went didn t really like it once he was old enough he dropped out and got a ged he didn t think college was for him but decided a career as a software developer was what he wanted and i guess he just taught himself figured it out he said it took a few jobs before people really took him seriously though after that most people don t care about degrees amp x b he s a really good developer i m trying to be fair and objective from my perspective i d say he and i are of comparable ability levels not that i m anything special i just mean all my classes and both of my degrees haven t made me more productive or more useful to the company', 'hmmm even as a bachelor you have a slight edge than others but opportunity cost is so high ie you ll be losing lots of money just going straight in a masters program with not much more guarantee of landing a job some people are lucky through work to get it covered so that is a much better option you haven t mentioned what job you want to exactly except a generic programming want to do web development create mobile apps create tools work in ai machine learning i ask this because some bachelors of cs wants to get into machine learning which currently does favor those with a masters ph d but probably by the time you graduate from your masters it probably won t matter currently for pretty much everything a bachelors in cs is enough to land you good career job to move up into management you need soft skills that no master degree program is going to teach you do you like meetings after meeting you ll have to learn to persuade other people not directly under you to do stuff your way you ll have to persuade higher up why your way is the best no matter how logical your plan is you ll have to fight tooth and nail why your plan is better than other people s plan do you like networking you can have a masters and still suck at soft skills so no a master will not help in your looking into management the other course is software architect which is a high level technical position but guess what the first requirement for them is the number of quality years of programming so doing a masters will delay you from achieving this position to be honest from what my friends explain most don t bother this route because their pay is already good and don t want the additional politics associated this higher positions so masters won t help you this way so where do a masters program help assuming you re attending already attending a decent state school is wait for it research i mean you want to spend all your time on a project that may or may not pan out imagine having your thesis destroyed because it didn t work all the time and effort and you ll have to start from scratch it is not easy to create a new idea but from your discussion it doesn t seem like this is what you have in mind but yes doing research in your undergrad will definitely help formulate what you ll be researching in for the master program guess what it an unsaid question you must answer in your master s application what do you want to research lastly there is another master program less research and more technical this one does help people who came from terrible cs program or like me who graduated from another degree other than bachelors of computer science or currently you really really want to do machine learning this again won t give you an edge for me by the time i graduate others who had a bachelors and working will have at minimum two years of the real world experience two years of invaluable experience and learning that is two years more towards a senior engineer developer we will all start at the bottom except maybe the masters will have slight edge picking their team but most probably not business needs mostly determine that but with two years under your belt you can bet you can probably transition to a better team that you re interested in or move to another company so masters didn t help here either if you re all set to get a master to go for it just don t go because of your parent s expectations that it will give you an edge moving up my cousin went for masters as an mba in a top tier school he is currently unemployed with huge student debt because now he has an opposite problem no one can afford him he did it too because his parents thought it would give him an edge on hindsight it would ve been better if he found a workplace to sponsor him because that company has already invested in him he would ve already had a career with that company so no a higher degree did not help quick answer internships and research will help you get masters get a job instead get company to pay for your masters would be the smartest thing to do but no the master will not give you a real edge in the promotion even if you re fully funded you ll be losing so much experience that it hurts do a masters with a career job and paid by the company is the most optimal thing you can do if you really must have a masters do you know what is worst going straight to the master program then going out to get a job realizing that your leader graduated the same time as you when you graduated with bachelors then find out that you hate real world coding that was another two years delay from finding out two years that you could ve used to transition to something else so for a very narrow niche reason yes a masters program would be right for you it is so narrow that it s pointy and it hurts since it already sounds like you re an educated person from a decent school it would be hard to recommend you to do masters and if you re still worried about parent pressure though at this point you re an adult tell them that the sooner you get a job the sooner you ll get experience towards becoming a senior engineer developer which will protect you and make you invaluable explain that waiting two years might mean you won t be able to land a job because job saturation from boot camps a white lie and being deemed too overqualified and expensive there are situations where having a ph d does hurt you though not so much cs this should have your parent off your back because the last thing they want is their child still living at home with no job they can google it all and find articles about how programming is becoming oversaturated most of it is misleading and overhyped to make news but it will at least convince your parents that real world experience is important remind them of aerospace engineers was once a really hot field oh how about mechanical engineers they should remember those time when they started seeing them working at mcdonald s']

Name: 5_internship_summer_internships_intern
Representative_Docs: ['gt so in essence it seems like internships are less risky and lower cost propositions for companies to train potentially new hires and discover a rough diamond employee who they can polish to their mold that is indeed correct gt so shouldn t more companies be willing to take on interns then if not why do so many people struggle just to even get a single internship the demand should be quite strong for interns no hiring an intern often takes as much time in the hiring process as hiring a full time employee this represents a significant investment in finding a person as evidenced by the renege and keep looking attitude this is also a risky proposition it s quite possible that the person you ve selected decides to go elsewhere instead next there s the investment of time once the intern is brought on an intern easily takes h week of senior fte resources this then means those projects that the developer is working on are going to be completed slower and no the intern will not make that happen any faster next well so you ve decided to invest in hiring an intern you ve hired one you lost invested the equivalent of one month of senior developer time into the intern and they re a dud you don t offer a fte position back to them and move on but you still spent a few tens of thousands of dollars finding this out between the intern pay for the summer and the loss of productivity on the team could have gone a significant way along the path of hiring a regular fte employee and lastly there s the job hop every year or so attitude that many junior developers have you ve found someone invested the summer in them offered a position they accepted and a year later they ve left and are at a different place you know what hiring interns sucks and is a not insignificant risk that it won t pan out it s an opportunity if you have the resources in staffing and sufficient slack in the schedule and are willing to train someone for the chance that they re into physics i made the switch to cs about two weeks ago in my spare time i have been taking an intro course and a course on data structures and algorithms on coursera i got an offer to do particle physics research at one of the department of energy national labs this summer and while i was previously ecstatic to do it i am not sure if it makes sense to continue with the internship i really want to focus my time on building a good foundation in cs developing quality projects for my portfolio and practicing for the coding interview this summer to prepare for tech recruiting this fall that will be quite difficult when the majority of my time is spent doing research i am quite concerned that spending that much time on research will just leave me uncompetitive for tech this upcoming recruiting cycle for summer i also just don t care for doing physics anymore the research will involve data analysis with python which is good experience but it definitely won t give me close to the amount of experience i can do with myself last summer i also had a research position which involved data analysis in python so i already have a data analysis background and am not sure another summer of doing that will do me any good i could put some time into trying to find a tech internship this summer either in software or data science through alum connections at smaller places in my hometown there are still a bunch of positions open on my school s career site and google jobs glassdoor etc but it is quite late for summer internships so not sure if this would be a waste of time if i could manage to swing a tech internship this summer should i drop my summer gig if i don t manage to find a tech internship this summer more likely would i be better off doing the physics research internship or just developing my portfolio and deepening my portfolio on my own the research position sounds impressive so maybe just having it to make me look smart is a good idea thanks everyone', 'updated from last year summer internship recruiting starts in late august before some of you even start college i wish someone had told me that as a freshman although hiring continues all year and some freshman specific programs open in winter if you re interested in software engineering these two guides should cover you how to land a top notch tech internship and a tech job while you re still in school a very detailed overview of the cs recruiting process from internships to your first job if you re a regular lurker at cscareerquestions you ll somewhat know most of it but if you re starting from or near it s invaluable internship hunting as an underclassman i wrote this guide targeted at st and nd year students primarily community college students it s mostly a collection of useful terms and Name: 6_math_algebra_calculus_programming
Representative_Docs: ['that depends for a computer science degree you re going to need to complete quite a bit of math at a minimum you re going to need to complete calculus i and ii discrete math and probability and statistics you may also have to complete linear algebra for computer science courses programming courses only really requires basic algebra but computer science is more than just programming you ll have to take a courses on data structures and algorithms as well as computing theory which has quite a bit of math involved but nothing too complicated you ll also have to take a course on computer organization computer hardware which goes into boolean algebra boolean logic but honestly i found boolean algebra easier to understand than regular algebra outside of those few courses there s not terribly much more math involved if you know basic to college level algebra you should be fine with computer science courses the math you need for your classes you can learn while you take them however if you aren t strong at math you may have a hard time getting through your degree since there s a bunch of advanced math courses you ll be required to take how much math you ll need also depends on what career you want to go into if you wanted to go into ai ml or data science you d need quite a bit of math but if you just want to become a software engineer you won t need to learn that much', 'it s good to think about the different branches of math and how they re useful that ll allow more focus than just more math amp x b continuous math algebra gt calculus gt differential equations for legacy reasons this is the complete and total focus of most high school and university math courses to the extant that when people say math they are actually thinking continuous math algebra is vital for programming variables and handy for general life calculus and differential equations not so much though calculus is so widely assumed that it s hard to get thorough understandings of some other branches without it amp x b corollary the math taught for a standard engineering degree isn t all that on target for most programming and cs degrees often get the same curriculum by default an enormous amount of it is for solving physics problems amp x b statistics this perhaps ties algebra as the most useful for everyday life also good if you re talking with non programmers it s widely used in business generally the foundation of machine learning amp x b discrete math combinatorics integer math a cs degree will probably have a course in this and even though the math isn t really that difficult it s so different from all the other math classes that it can be a weedout class very useful for programming and essential for algorithms amp x b linear algebra matrices vectors typically this comes in two fragments matrix basics in algebra ii and then again at the very end of an engineering undergrad it s not an ideal way to approach the subject which gives it a tough reputation but it doesn t actually require calculus required for any sort of numeric programming computer vision modeling graphics amp x b geometry trigonometry vectors projective geometry computer vision robotics and game design i think you can skip trig if you aren t doing this sort of work amp x b so i d say the two fundamentals for programming are algebra and discrete math start on those two then follow up with statistics but do keep in mind that self studying is hard and most people click more on some things than others having fun going deep in lt geometry gt will be far more rewarding than getting frustrated with lt statistics gt', 'being good at math really does just make you better at programming my college also had a lot of logic classes we could take under philosophy and i packed my social sciences with those and it really showed me so much overlap between math logic and computer science a good analogy is that math and computer science are both applied variants of logic similar to how physics is applied math they are very similar both different versions of the same applied subject they both have the same root so being add at one will help you in some way with the other that being said i have actually used just about everything i have learned in my math classes in programming algebra this is a given linear algebra computer graphics if you ever want to learn how to make a game i have dabbled in my spare time but i am certainly not in the industry this will be very very helpful discrete math the proof theory and problem solving you learn in this class should apply to your programming every day set math helps a ton with data structures set data structures are a thing and they do have use cases in programming stats this one is a stench i have not really used any of the probability tests but i imagine some machine learning or business intelligence or other related cs focus could use them a lot the general probabilities and data modeling is useful for handling data and determining general risk assessment which is something applies to most fields calculus differential equations this is the one that most generally not the most helpful but some things do use it like if you build a d game engine you will need this a lot of d math either will use this or linear algebra depending on what types of calculations you need to make however an important thing about calculus is that it is always a gateway into other forms of higher math like discrete math and linear algebra at least from what i have seen so this stuff is usually required so you can learn the stuff that really important to cs']

Name: 7_ml_learning_machine learning_ai ml
Representative_Docs: ['basically what happened was that google ponied up and open sourced their optimized c and cuda code with an easy to use python library called tensorflow that was less than years ago after that happened things have been getting really crazy in the data science field it s not just the models it s also because of the huge increase in available data there is a famous ml paper called the unreasonable effectiveness of data from awhile ago the entire idea behind the paper is that if you have enough data even a super simple model ends up getting really good better than a really complex model with less data now whether the increase in data or the advancements in models or the increase in hardware power is responsible for the initial ml boom i m not sure but i do suspect that a lot of why machine learning is getting so big now with so much academic focus and corporate funding is because models have been shown to be actually feasible and profitable you see although neural networks are actually a pretty old idea as in they were first thought of in the s no one thought it was actually possible to train one until someone came up with a good backpropagation algorithm in the s things sort of kept going slowly up through the s and early s when there was more focus on support vector machines than deep learning i think then i guess the internet got big and smartphones got big and so data got big and now everyone carries around their phones everywhere so data is even more huge and we got enough computer power and data for deep learning models to do useful things and tensorflow got released and now everything is moving very very fast', 'decision trees linear and logistic regression are all very common tools probably even more so than deep learning but deep learning is used very intentionally for a lot of the heavy lifting in ml applications deep learning is most appropriate when you have practically infinite compute resources e g server side and performance is the most important concern but it may not be best if you care about explainability or have strict resource limits e g speed latency battery life disk space etc that s a shifting landscape as theory catches up to practice in neural networks and better technology develops to support deep learning on mobile but it will basically always be true that a small linear model is faster and more compact than a state of the art deep learning model in production ml the goal is to make a useful feature or system and to do so you make trades between many different constraints in research ml the goal is to get published so it s most important to outperform a benchmark in order to show that your technique represents an improvement if i can get of the performance of a state of the art deep learning solution from a well tuned linear model then i m definitely going to at least start by building the feature around a linear model we can always go back and replace it with deep learning but the linear model is easier and faster to build and test and the marginal improvement is probably unnecessary', 'take this with a grain of salt i have dabbled in ml and understand it moderately well but in my opinion it s not an exhaustive technology and moreover we are at the limits of application of learning and deep learning algorithms more plainly it seems like there are a finite small number of actual ml learning models whose node weights can change based on a given training set but the problem they re suited for solving is defined and finite concretely let s think about image recognition the same trained neural net that can detect pictures of cat can also be trained to recognize you the underlying ml model the neural network is the same is discovered and is in use the only thing that changes are node weights as the new training model is processed but the underlying algorithm is a known quantity yes they can do improve but we will find leaders in the space that can do this better than of home grown attempts given any purveyor of ml s ability to see many many many use cases to me this is pretty clear that it s the training sets that are going to be more valuable but this is the most low tech portion of ml algorithms as it s generally humans curating training lists going through pictures and saying cat true cat true cat false in correlation with the target image there are now ml algorithms that can speed some of this up but it doesn t detract from the point that we are fished out andrew ng one of the first to provide training material in ml to the public agrees that the business value of ml has shifted from algorithms to training sets i can t find the video but if you press me i will so if you agree that the set of ml networks in use are finite then businesses can and will specialize and provide expertise that will be extremely hard to replicate in a smaller company amazon is at the forefront with services like sagemaker which take away a ton of the pain of managing the frameworks for ml they ve even procured specialized processors to speed up training and they ve introduced opinionated ml algorithms for various contexts which again might shock you how finite they are that can be used towards any modern application they also allow you to optimize your algorithms see which inputs are most correlated to desired outputs possibly cull from the training set which is largely the most difficult part of creating a ml algorithm the tweaking to boot amazon has opened up a marketplace for ml models some with training sets some without which will help speed up the commoditization of a finite set of solutions in sum it s going to be the application of known ml algorithms in conjunction with training sets that drives value into the future until some new means of ai comes along which i don t think is on the horizon there s certainly a case to be made of the power of ml trained sets working in concert being able to fulfill the sci fi fantasies of ai you could probably argue that humans are very likely the equivalent of millions perhaps billions of running ml learning algorithms after all ml is modeled after neurons you can probably guess that this is computationally bound and will be for quite a while i have no idea how many ml trained networks run in an auto driving tesla but i d be super shocked if it exceeds each network has a job pedestrian id other car id gps navigation road identification etc etc etc point being it s already happening that ml as a science is being dumbed down and products are being offered that largely cut out the need to be on the cutting edge of new types of ml algorithms as the set of problems is finite i do believe there will always be space for new algorithms but it will and is shrinking most companies that are innovating are actually using long established ml models such as for robotics etc applied to their specific use case differentiated by training sets though not by ml algorithms hope this made sense happy to expand on anything if what i m saying is not clear']

Name: 8_interview_questions_interviews_leetcode
Representative_Docs: ['i haven t had my interview yet so i don t have any interview questions to share i did however have a phone interview before getting invited to the final round this consisted of talking to a principal data scientist who asked questions about my resume particularly my most recent internship what i did pressed me on my deep learning experience what conflicts i faced and how i resolved them and what my experience working in a team was like the last few minutes were allocated for me to ask questions', 'i just got an offer from nvidia for an applied research position the process was very fast i got a first phone interview then a second interview on the next week then the final offer i didn t have an hr interview or no site both of my interviews were technical the first one was more basic knowledge about programming question machine and deep learning and cuda related questions the second one had programming questions and practical deep learning questions let me know if you need any help with the interview good luck', 'tldr long ancedote rant about how you can get software engineering roles without any of this leetcode bullshit i wish i knew this sooner since i spent so much time studying shit i didn t and would never need as a college student in cs most finance investment insurance places like in ny city hardly ask any technical questions pure softball zero leetcode i ve done dozens of interviews for game companies and several big n interviews it was a struggle for me in college to juggle both because they are fundamentally different in hiring for a generalist gameplay or engine role most game company squeeze math algorithms and practical technical questions out you rapid fire in screeners they re extremely direct and on the nose explain a d plane projection algorithm what s x insert random power of multiply x number without bitshifting and with bitshifting here s a class header what s its size in bytes here s a whacky code snippet of c what s it doing and what s its output given x hardware requirements explain how d you design a heap allocator in detail explain and perform endian swapping of course if you re doing ai or graphics or networking ontop of the above you ll get a ton more hyper specific questions like in networking explain how d you byte pack these integers and how d you d optimize this or that payload but the big n companies are asking generic brain teasers and generic string manipulation searching no matter which position i applied for it was really difficult trying to juggle both types of interviews while also in school there was also the third type of interview i had to prepare for too which was the generic student interviews that companies like morgan stanley or usaa would do like group assignments and shit like how d you design elevators or deal with a bad coworkers super easy to do softball questions but for me personally if that s the core of the interview it completely would throw me off and even when i nailed it i had no idea what the actual job would be like so i started turning them down i was hard core studying d algebra and calculus for hours in the beginning of the day then some specialization like networking or hardware architecture midday and end of day i d have to unwind with brain teasers and geeksforgeeks or whatever book of big n interview questions then and only then did i allow myself time for school work virtually did no actual programming and don t get me started on jumping from a big n interview to a game interview to something soft like a bank interview or vice versa back to back mental gynamistics i choked on more than interview because i just did a completely different type of interview prior anyways i got multiple offers at varying times throughout college from companies like ms rockstar morgan stanley the only interviews i came out of knowing well what i d be working on and how i d feel working on was the game companies every time and not a single one of them ever asked me anything i d seen as a leet code hard or any of the crazy questions i ve seen floating around this sub and other cs subs i wish i knew this sooner on because i would ve dropped that nonsense much sooner and just focused more on the math hardware and actually programming stuff like network stacks apis or ais i also would ve had much more time in college to actually do better and have fun and those softball interviews at companies like morgan stanley or fidelity well a lot of my friends ended up working at those companies and they not only make more money than me but they do like a fraction of the work too and have way comfier setups with less stress i m saying this because most of them couldn t do easy leetcode questions but they all have well paying jobs and have held them well after college and are happier than i ve ever been when i read half the questions on this sub and others i m shocked to hear people basically living and dying by this shit when several of my very not good engineer friends have been quite successful sometimes i do stop and wonder was all that time frustration disappointment being locked up on my room or library worth it for the job s i got offers for sometimes i don t know i feel better about myself for getting through the challenges learning cool shit getting to work on stuff like red dead redemption but i look at some of my college buddies and whistle at their sweet setups that took comparatively waaaaay less headache and effort no big n companies no crazy hardware or math knowledge no leetcode']

Name: 9_resume_bullet_section_did
Representative_Docs: ['it s good to consider readability however as someone who s junior to the industry your biggest enemy is whitespace and you should avoid using it your resume s target audience is non technical recruiters and hr reps lack of content tends to equate to lack of ability if even only subconsciously a resume with words which should be your target word count can still remain readable the tricks are place the most important proper nouns at the front of the sentence shrink the line height for wrapped list item text while keeping separate list item lines padded using horizontal rules as part of your headings utilizing different fonts preferably a sans and a sans serif font selection and different grayscale colors for headings my biggest tip regarding your layout is you should definitely not place your headings on a separate column that generates a significant amount of whitespace that takes away from the meat of your resume anyway for some additional resume advice move skills to the top and education to the bottom for your bullet points don t summarize responsibilities talk about specific actions and specific achievements don t summarize or list technologies focus on specific achievements with each technology and incorporate them into the descriptions every single bullet point should be about a technology you used otherwise the whole bullet point should be cut don t use jargon like nadam adam or pos that a non technical recruiter won t see on their list of words to look out for so tell what how you provisioned a resource with aws lambda tell me what methods you used to create the django rest api tell me why you chose bootstrap over vanilla css as an intern this is the pattern for bullet points that i usually recommend for people what you used how you used it what the feature was why you used it this way who benefited from it in the end e g wrote python with django to lt what methods in django you used gt to build a rest endpoint utilizing lt why you used those methods in django gt so as to lt why anyone benefited from your actions gt built react components using lt what approach tools or methods gt to create lt what type of front end interactive forms a data visualization tool gt so as to lt why you did it this way or why you used those tools gt vastly improving lt how this benefited the team or end users gt contributed python to a machine learning library using lt what code did you write or methods approach did you take gt to write lt how would you briefly describe a nadam optimizer to a layman gt utilizing lt why a nadam optimizer actually optimizes the library gt vastly improving the lt what performance gt performance by every bullet point should try to hit these items including those in your projects and research sections', 'for rephrasing the bullet points take this approach start with a proper noun preferably something that a non tech recruiter would recognize explain what you did with it explain why you used it as opposed to some other technology explain what the end benefit was so gt devised a combinatorial algorithm gt designed an programmed an efficient python implementation gt showcased algorithm at the these are three pieces to what should be a single bullet point but you re still missing what benefit python bought you here also was this the only thing you did in this position if not you need more bullet points if it was the only thing you did here then i d move it to your projects section gt created a monte carlo simulation in c utilized c to simulate the monte carlo computational algorithm leveraging the former to lt reason why you needed to use c gt thereby modeling neutron energy dispositions for lt whoever benefited from this gt gt examined the gb of resulting data using how did you use python what is a root library was there any software involved who benefited from this gt utilized mit s distributed computing resources with what technology how did you utilized it who benefited from the simulations gt presented analysis biweekly move this line plus the division of nuclear physics line over to a new section call it recent talks and add these as blurbs to it gt contributed to the design of a low cost cosmic ray detector using what technology how did you contribute what was the design how much more affordable was the new design gt wrote extensive documentation were the models and analytics specific to any technologies were there any patterns you utilized in writing the docs how extensive was this documentation gt succeeded in reducing detector materials how with what technology gt used an iterative machine learning program wrote c scripts to feed lt the database you were using gt via lt how you collected the data gt to machine learning lt un supervised gt models by lt why you used c in the first place gt to calibrate particle collisions on behalf of lt whoever benefited from this gt that reminds me have you used any databases at all you should definitely add them to your descriptions regarding sections move skills to the top skills only have two purposes on a resume to keyword stack a computer analyzer and to position a reader s expectations regarding your job experience the former doesn t care where you put it but the latter requires you to at least place skills above experience if you want it to matter move education to the bottom split your education and recent talks section which i suggested above into two columns on the same row add an interests section after education at the bottom interests help to humanize people it should only be one or two lines max and you should avoid anything political or racial remove the study abroad entry entirely or if you really want to talk about it move it to your new interests section additionally project descriptions should be handled just like the job descriptions use the same four point pattern that i mentioned above with only skills they should only take up one line nix the labels and just keep them as a set of comma separated tags i didn t see any r java or latex in any of your descriptions if you don t incorporate either the language itself or its libraries into your descriptions then i m just going to assume that you re lying about knowing these languages the keywords in your computer science coursework should be moved to skills skills can also be padded out with more libraries if you ve used them databases and testing frameworks too remove the gpa from education remove the months from your dates just leave them as years add years to your projects as mentioned above treat them exactly as you would a job description that s about all i can think of cheers', 'descriptions amp bullet points are the currency that gets you a callback these are what you should dedicate the most resume space towards meaning cut the whitespace on honors make projects a full width section and collapse the skills section to three or four lines at most regarding the skills section i d recommend moving it to the top to prime the reader s expectations cut the lines coded blurbs since ranking your knowledge here is never a good idea if you don t feel comfortable talking about a tech language i would leave it off this section it s okay for these languages to mentioned elsewhere on the resume the next section should be your work experience be explicit and specific about the work you performed the more you merely summarize your work the weaker a candidate you ll appear to a recruiter relative to your peers you re not competing against a job description your resume is in a fight against other resumes so you want to avoid being as generic as they are right now write each line with this pattern what i used how i used it why using it was necessary in the context of the project or what problem was solved by my solution and optionally who benefited from my solution example wrote react native modules with lt how i built or approached my code gt so that lt why you built or approached it this way or why react native was a good gt example built node js api using lt how i built or approach it gt utilizing lt why you built it this way or why you chose node js gt vastly improving lt who benefited from this and why gt etc full stack development can easily be split into three or more lines on its own go full try hard in writing descriptions for your most recent position lines is totally doable recruiters and hiring managers tend to focus their attention the most here so this is the place to be as impressive as possible always name drop at least one technical proper noun for each bullet point performed basic statistical analysis using what python say it performed groundwork research on artificial intelligence ecosystems using what technologies name them as a recent graduate you want to support as many technologies named in your skills as possible skills shouldn t be the only place i see something as big as c don t make me take your word for it tell me where you used these technologies and how avoid using words like basic the projects section should be next style this exactly as you did the work experience section make it full width use bullet points and use the same description pattern that i mentioned above for education cut the honors awards scholarships gpa and coursework you re a career engineer now not a college intern instead talk about the most interesting projects you contributed to describe them using the same pattern as i mentioned above the hackathon project is a good thing to talk about don t just say you won one name the technologies and how you achieved a solution cut the mailing address']

Name: 10_learning_machine learning_machine_deep learning
Representative_Docs: ['it depends if you really want a good understanding or if you just want to be able to implement some machine learning ml models if you really want to understand what is going on how the models are created structured trained and used then you will need a mathematical understanding first i would recommend getting a solid grasp on statistics with a focus on probability gaussian bayes etc set theory knowing this will make linear algebra far more digestible linear algebra vectors matrices etc calculus this is less important and is mostly used more sparingly an understanding of basic differentiation and integration will be useful though machine learning is quite a broad field with lots of different approaches and applications mostly these falls into three categories supervised learning used to classify items into predefined categories requires labelled training data showing correct classification for some example data a good starting point would be linear regression some more complex but commonly used algorithms include neural networks and support vector machines unsupervised learning used to group items together based on similarities often called clustering requires a training set of data but does not required labels a good starting point would be k means or k nearest neighbour reinforcement learning used to select actions based on the state of the environment and a reward given these models are often used to control robotics or games without requiring any training data the models learn by doing the best start here would be to look into q learning in terms of what languages and libraries to use i would recommend python or r as both have a good range of available tools there are a lot of libraries out there which can make building these models easy and require no real understanding of the math however without the background understanding you can miss what is really happening fast ai is a really good starting point the library is quite simple and there are some really good accompanying videos to describe the process and reasoning behind some of the builds keras gives more freedom but holds your hand a little less requiring you to be able to read the documentation and understand the underlying structure of the models tensorflow is the internals of keras it gives you quite low level control of your models but requires a further level of understanding in terms of getting started i would recommend learning the basics of a language like python first so that you are confident programming in that language in general then i would recommend you pick a specific area of machine learning and investigate deeper learning the relevent math as you progress best of luck hope this gives you a good starting point', 'like the other poster mentioned ml is not a beginner topic and thus there really aren t ml for beginners type courses available that will do the subject due justice instead there are a variety of subjects you should aim to master before tackling ml and application pasted below is something i recently posted in regards to how to approach learning ml as an addendum the vast majority of ai ml roles require at the very least a masters degree in statistics computer science or another hard science with ml experience they prefer projects over software development internships although an internship will not hurt and even then many roles require at least a phd if you want the understand machine learning inside and out then the subject areas you want to focus on in order of importance statistics linear algebra strang gilbert linear algebra and it s applications is a great resource differential calculus if you just want to be able to use apply and be able to fluently talk about machine learning then focus on statistics linear algebra as far as differentiation is concerned it s used by researchers to create different methods of learning and regression it s highly likely you don t need to concern yourself with how the equations you ll used were derived nor do you need to understand the math behind them what you do need to know is what different machine learning algorithms methods do and what their pros cons are what different learning equations do and what the pros cons of those are in respect to learning rates of neural networks how to clean data cut down on bias in your data and create your own data apply different ml algorithms to different scenarios interpret results statistics will allow you to do and linear algebra allows you to do and knowing what different linear algebra operations do will help you understand and write better code past that everything is pretty much statistics based understanding how the dimensions of your data need to relate is all linear algebra this is why it s important for the coding aspect of ml you need to know how to get from step a to step b while keeping things consistent the hardest and most important thing to learn in regards to mastering ml is data manipulation data is the most important thing when it comes to ml', 'if you want the understand machine learning inside and out then the subject areas you want to focus on in
