practical approach at this point or a more theoretical you also need to learn a data science programming language r or python maybe java i ll recommend r it s not good but the best there is more years now you ll be read to do basic data science then you ll need to learn about all the pitfalls there are many and tricks this takes years if in addition you want to write your own machine learning algorithms you ll need learn matematical programming focus on convex optimization hence you also need to learn convex analysis if you want to be a pro there is a lot more to learn at this point it s matematics learn a low level programming language and learn it good recommended is c forget cpp i made the mistake of using too much time learning all the ins and outs of cpp use years making your first machine learning algorithm package library a lot of work can be fun at times though', 'others have already stated what you need well unsounded gave a great response amp x b i will add this to help you understand what level of math skills you need amp x b i have an undergraduate degree in finance not mathematics my degree required ish stats classes a couple calculus courses a couple econometrics etc so i have done a bit of math lets say university level math courses from my level of math understanding i can determine what is happening at a basic level with different ml techniques in scikit learn i generally get the benefits of each technique and generally when to use them basically i know just enough to be able to use scikit learn or other ml libraries to get decent conclusions out of data would i ever win a competition for model accuracy probably not would i ever be able to get a job working in advanced ai at a big tech company i havent tried but i doubt i would ever be able to without more advanced knowledge of the math maybe i could get a job using ml in a lighter setting but mostly its for personal projects and knowledge levels you can use libraries like tensorflow sklearn and actually use ml on data without much math but you wont really be able to get jobs or apply it in really effective ways most likely be at my level of understanding and use stated above you ve read intro level textbooks on la stats diff calc if you want a job in advanced ai ml start reading university level math textbooks because you will need lots of it start with intro textbooks like into to stats or into to diff calc etc then move on to the intermediate and advanced reads amp x b maybe someone with an actual job in ml will say different but this has been my understanding so far in learning ml also eightieskid has it right khan academy then and coursera udemy and kaggle are great resources also look at amp x b its is a good basic overview of the math in ml but it may not be the easiest read without prior knowledge in stats la calc if you read it and think wtf are they talking about then start with khan academy or an intro stats book also dont be intimidated a lot of the stuff i read i think wtf are they talking about and need youtube or etc to catch up', 'mathematics scalars vectors and tensors are the heart of machine learning tensors lend themselves quite well to data representation for example a picture might be represented as a tensor of order three where each pixel has corresponding r g b values it s extremely important to have a basic understanding of what tensors represent and how to manipulate them using linear algebra techniques n spaces and higher dimensional space one of the hardest parts of machine learning for me was visualizing data in dimensions past n three because we vectorize our complicated data it often exists in a large multidimensional space it s important to understand higher dimensional spaces and that we are not limited to three dimensions despite how we see and visualize real space partial derivatives and gradients are used for activation functions and a variety of optimization functions we often use the inverse gradient of a function to determine how weights are adjusted to minimize error to perform gradient calculations you will need to know how to take partial derivatives of a function regression is used to find lines of fit for data and determine how great an effect each point has on a final outcome a brief explanation of regression can be found here mathematical logic logic gates and truth tables are the core of perceptron algorithms they compare two conditions using binary flags here is a list of common logic gates and their i o probability theory thank you u kittttttens when i was learning to write ml code we used stochastic matrices to represent probability that said machine learning is an incredibly powerful statistical analysis tool and the statistics used in the algorithms would warrant a section of its own for starters the classes i ve taken have touched on linear regression logistic regression clustering and other similar topics i m not well versed in this though i would gladly add to my post if somebody did a more thorough explanation python libraries numpy is a mathematics tool for python it contains simple calls for complex mathematical operations such as finding the gradient of a surface multiplying vectors or taking the average of multiple data numpy has incredible documentation pandas allows for data to be read into python we often work with csv files and pandas is the translator that allows us to read them in this is the most intuitive explanation of pandas i have yet found it is very powerful and relatively straightforward matplotlib allows data to be presented with charts and plots this is essential for communicating the results of your algorithm their website has a very helpful list of tutorials seaborn is an extension of matplotlib with fantastic tools such as a correlation heatmap their website also has decent documentation tensorflow and keras are a fantastic comprehensive ml library built by google many of the standard ml libraries are built straight into tf eliminating the need to write tedious mathematical processes because it is such a popular and well used library their website has fantastic documentation python skills list comprehension is vital for writing good ai ml code the processes are complicated therefore it s best to simplify loops whenever possible it is also a good practice for coding in general a tutorial can be found here garbage collecting is done automatically by python but manual gc can still improve performance in larger high cost algorithms please don t hesitate to make suggestions or additions to the list hopefully it serves as a starting point for those of you looking to explore ai ml programming']

Name: 33_bootcamp_section_bootcamps_instructors
Representative_Docs: ['i recently graduated from the university of minnesota trilogy data analytics amp visualization boot camp i highly recommend the program but only if you are serious about wanting to learn it s a time commitment outside of class a lot of studying tinkering reviewing solutions on stack overflow and w schools com etc did i learn a lot the first day of class i knew very little about coding didn t even know what bash was i had acted as a product manger for some software development projects did my fair share of software testing looking for bugs but didn t know how to write any code so i was familiar with some of the lingo and processes but didn t know how to write a single line of code my final project was machine learning classifying sec k risk factor text documents for a professor at the u a side project i did was scrapping tweets and performing a sentiment analysis for a unc professor i learned a ton be prepared for class class is fast paced classes had two components instructor led examples and student in class activities that was a combined in class activities in months the activities are where i learned a lot it s practicing what you were just taught and then getting immediate feedback on the right way to complete the activity a lot of my learning was figuring out why my code wasn t working then making sure i didn t continue to make the same syntax mistakes we had homework assignments and projects they give you the option to complete the easy intermediate or hard assignments i always did the hard assignments every one of them was challenging and took hours to complete positives instructors there were two cohorts that met on separate nights during the week and together on the weekends the instructors would alternate for the combined saturday class both were data scientists by day and very knowledgeable both on their own time would post articles job openings and other resources to the cohort slack channel instructors are probably a big reason why trilogy has some good and bad reviews i got lucky having two great instructors that took an interest in making sure we were learning we also had great tas maybe our classes just got lucky not a single person dropped out of the class between the two classes people made it the entire months and graduated surveys made a difference every week you are required to fill out a class survey to access your student portal for the first months every week i responded please give us a syllabus trilogy never gave us a syllabus but our instructor finally started slacking out what we were going to be covering in the upcoming week very helpful i saw this week on our class page that trilogy posted a syllabus for future cohorts what s my point i felt that they listened to student feedback and made incremental changes throughout the months this might not be the case at every university but the umn trilogy success manager and the instructors seemed to take the feedback and tried to implement improvements along the way class videos every class session is recorded so you can watch it later the first few months i didn t use that often but when the content started getting more difficult and i referred to the videos quite a bit negatives they didn t provide a syllabus this was frustrating because i wanted to spend time preparing at home so when i heard about a new function in class it wouldn t be the first time career services isn t what i thought it was when i signed up but it has been satisfactory after i adjusted my expectations career services is meant to get you interview ready not a placement service that being said a few weeks before graduation they announced that we would be having a demo days optional for students that would be interested in participating there are quite a few decent companies pre registered to attend us bank united health care wells fargo thomson reuters best buy etc the admissions process was a little confusing and misleading many of the students didn t realize until the st day of class that this was a trilogy education program not umn the admissions people used umn edu emails but once class started the support emails were all trilogy we took classes at the u but we weren t umn students that being said my certificate says university of minnesota in big font in partnership with trilogy education services in smaller font and is signed by umn deans misleading at the beginning but i got the umn certificate at the end what was most surprising how much we learned in such a short period of time i wouldn t consider myself an expert or fluent in the languages libraries we were exposed to but i did learn how to find answers examples library supplements etc for example now i feel very comfortable importing connecting to a data set csv sql data lake api web scraping gt cleaning normalizing the data and performing statistical analysis gt creating a variety of visualizations linked to the data gt creating website dashboards and api for people to explore the data i was also surprised at how time was spent on html css javascript and browser platforms flask when you think about it we learned how to take huge data millions of rows visualize it with cool interactive visuals look at to become inspired and excited so it makes sense that we would want to learn how to make it easy for non programming people to access did i consider alternatives to the trilogy boot camp yes however there weren t many options in minnesota for classroom instruction which is what i was interested in i took a few classes on coursera so i could have some additional activities to work on python for everybody was a helpful it went a little more in depth with some of the python functions but didn t come close to covering as much content as trilogy i also watched a lot of videos on coreyms com and dataschool io those were great supplements and again offered more activities to practice with my advice ask some questions about the instructors are they data scientists do they use python daily etc if time allows read and do some of the exercises in learn python the hard way before the boot camp starts make sure you have make sure you have plenty of time outside of class for homework and practicing', 'overview these trilogy programs reviews and boot camps in general are so rarely reviewed that i wanted to drop my experience on one that just completed in may i will jump into tons of super detailed and nitty gritty stuff as this review continues however i just wanted to start with an outline first of all i think trilogy has a smart business plan to leverage the brands of trusted universities well until the recent scandal and their idle space thus skirt the need to build a strong brand however i think trilogy also comes across somewhat as a scam here as i hadn t even heard trilogy services until way later it was pretty terrible in the sense that it was not clear who the course s instructors would be clear they were not hired by the university i was enrolling through and eventually revealed that the program is not accredited through the university s accreditation process meaning it may have tax implications for you if work isn t paying for it and that no one external has ever reviewed either material or instructor second this class used to be a data visualization class and it shows as after weeks of useful material this course takes a strange u turn into middle of nowhere land you start using and learning things that you would never be able to use at any job ever they re fun it is not practical and it will make you wonder if you are in the right course plotly really heroku flask leaflet tons of pointless stuff and wasted time as python becomes a distant memory also every time you login into the course you see the lovely data visualization logo that they haven t bothered to update along with everything else if you are wondering why mba programs still use case studies from the s and s look no further as that will be what trilogy looks like in a few years as their material gets more and more dated so why did i select this course structure it is so easy to say i will self learn but then it s daunting to even start as someone with zero coding background it s nice to have small broken up pieces to get the ball rolling i really felt overwhelmed by the material out there and had no idea where to begin and no accountability to keep going second it provides a strong foundation where you can easily ask questions of an instructor when you are confused or wonder what the industry best practices are it s hard to learn something when there are cracks in your foundations so i wanted to interact with a real professional in order to get a base line before venturing off on my own third time wise i think this course delivers material at a fast pace in a short period of time but long enough that cross languages concepts start to make sense even if the implemented syntax is different the problem is that i think any true university master s course would go too slow most of the other advertised courses free or otherwise may go fast enough and deeper but will you end up with an understanding of the concepts or an expected route that they think you ve memorized this real job prospects do i think i could be employed just from having taken this course hell no do i think i could learn enough on the job from having taken this course probably but that s due to my immense background in math and analysis prior and not due to the course s proper preparation so everyone wants to know what real job prospects are like after this while i think people will be able to find work how many people in my own cohort do i think should really be a hirable talent at best and if one is truly being picky for entry level data analyst work probably not good odds this course is meant to be for everyone and anyone who has done anything in the business world knows a product meant for everyone is really refined for no one they could easily have doubled the course length and material by really expanding time spent in python and pandas it would be extremely helpful for anyone hoping to get a job but the problem is they never spent any time figuring out who their customer is trilogy has been growing at such a breakneck pace they didn t have to they also could easily have Name: 34_ml_jobs_roles_research
Representative_Docs: ['gt can someone explain the field and how where to get into a project or how to get a job doing projects that involve ml you are confusing two types of ml jobs one is straight up academic career research scientist jobs where you are publishing results i m talking about academic career in reference to research career in general whether they be in universities or in industry if you don t have a phd you can pretty much forget about research scientist jobs if you look at the types of people in these jobs a lot of them will have had done post docs or will be coming with an experience as an assistant professor in a university the other type of jobs is the regular machine learning engineer jobs sometimes labeled as software engineer machine learning you will not be doing groundbreaking research in these roles that s for the research scientists in ml engineer jobs you are taking some data that the company has and writing prediction learning algorithms so for instance content recommendation is a highly used use case of ml in a company i e how does yelp serve up new restaurants to try for its users for these jobs you are using libraries like scikit learn or tensorflow to write machine learning products that s being put to use for a business need you are not researching new ground breaking reinforcement learning algorithms that you are gonna present at icml that s what the research scientists do', 'it depends on what you want to do with machine learning even companies that specialize in ai have many roles many of which do not use machine learning front end how do you display present your product data engineer how do you acquire store and move your data ml infrastructure how are you training your models deploying them etc ml engineer cleaning and exploring data making models the rest of my post talks about how to get into ml engineering if you are interested in being ml adjacent like in roles based on your experience i think your best bet is ml infrastructure alternatively you can try to find a position that is a normal back end role which will occasionally use ml packages to solve specific problems or you could find a company that is trying to use ml for networking and pitch yourself as a subject matter expert if you are interested in ml engineer and want to spend most of your time looking at data and developing models it s going to be a tough climb i don t want to discourage you but i want to paint a realistic picture of what you re facing so you can make an informed decision in addition to programming you re going to want to be strong in statistics linear algebra calculus and modeling most of my coworkers have phds in physics economics or machine learning those that do not usually have a very strong background in the prerequisites from some other experience the reason for this is that it is very easy to make bad models and quite hard to make good models and without the background it can be hard to distinguish between them on top of that the world of ml is evolving quickly new state of the art algorithms are developed on a monthly basis keeping up by routinely reading papers takes a lot of work many young researchers are burning the candle at both ends all that said i work in a group that is specifically focused on developing ml software and technology it may be that a software group that occasionally uses ml to solve specific problems will be enough to scratch your itch if you have any other questions let me know', 'i think one thing maybe missed stucturally is that even with the upswell of ai ml based projects companies the number of ai specialists you need or even want too many cooks too many people who want to do kewl stuff but not gruntwork is not that high the reason being that any real ml project still tends to be ml and data infrastructure work an swe generalist who is excited to learn about ml but doesn t require a job where they do nothing but hparam fiddling is going to typically be much better at the latter and more excited to do it net for your real ml project and i use quotes because there are a lot of questionable projects out there you re hiring at a or or even more ratio ml swe generalist and then to compound this issue if you take a step back this organizational structure now looks a lot like many hierarchical organizations not necessarily in the sense that they are formally on top but from a functional product perspective i e that the rest of the swe team is effectively there to create leverage for the ml team what happens when you have a hierarchical team where a whole bunch of people are there to provide uplift to a much smaller number of people you tend to make sure that you really hire the best that you can for those small number of people because if they make errors in decision making then the work of everyone else is often invalidated this means you push toward hiring hyper skilled specialists winners keep winning not recent gatech ml master s grads further as alluded to above that gatech ml master s grad perhaps reasonably probably wants to be out there doing stuff with gpus and tf and pytorch not formalizing your kubernetes architecture to scale your data pipeline or whatever or at least that is the concern of any hiring manager this further encourages a bifurcation between hiring very high skilled ml specialists and swe generalists with the grads in the middle being left out note i am not saying that no one in the middle is not getting to work on real ml in the job environment i m merely describing trend lines that may be germane to what you think you are observing we see this issue in our hiring if you really want to focus just on pure ml then my hiring bar for you is probably unfortunately for you suddenly going to look a lot like what i d expect a phd or someone out of google brain to be able to bring to the table and i fully realize that this is unrealistic for most non phd grads sorry but if you want to be single threaded on your work then this is what i have to see we re admittedly a smaller startup if you re person eng team then of course you have higher ability to offer specialization but real opportunities like that are still limited for early career grads lastly making above even worse to some degree there is no such thing as a small ml project today as a broad generalization to do real ml you have to be sitting on top of a lot of data which implies a big project with real business impact big projects imply big teams which imply specialization leading us back to and']

Name: 35_ml_machine learning_machine_phd
Representative_Docs: ['machine learning research is hugely math heavy if you don t have a phd then that s not what you ll be doing though you ll be fine with whatever math you know both machine learning and frontend work will be in demand for a while advantage of machine learning is that it s more likely to drive profit at big companies advantage of frontend is that every company needs frontend devs you don t need a masters phd to do stuff like data cleaning running machine learning libraries making visualizations and stuff like that you do need it a phd for research unless you manage to get some kickass publications in your free time a masters helps but if you have experience then that s not as big a deal basically yeah most of the stuff you d likely be doing is just finding ways to pipe data around re format it run tools and make reports not as fun as developing features in an application for most people you work on whatever needs to be done need the model to be more accurate then you do that need to compare two approaches and report to decision makers then you do that etc your team will tell you what to do and you ll know when you re done', 'machine learning engineer isn t a standard title yet it can mean one of two things a very very small number of people actually work on developing new machine learning algorithms if you want to do that you probably need a phd in computer science which means spending years doing original machine learning research even then the jobs are really hard to come by everyone coming out of grad school wants to work on machine learning because it s the hot new thing in comparison plenty of software engineers know a little bit about machine learning and use established proven algorithms to solve problems to get a job doing this you really don t need anything more than any other software engineer on average i d say this sort of role usually requires a bachelor s in c s or equivalent but it s plausible to do this role without a year degree just some relevant experience in a nutshell you d just need to be a strong programmer with a good c s background in algorithms and data structures plus good math including probability and statistics then just a few short machine learning courses you could learn online amp x b', 'gt and be able to do projects on ml or big data etc step obtain a cs degree if you want to focus on theoretical aspects of machine learning creating new models optimizing existing ones finding new applications of machine learning then you will want a phd you can also go with an applied math degree instead if you prefer that why phd because ml in this case means research a lot of research attending conferences publishing your own papers and reading other ones all the time these are exactly the same skills you learn in academia at phd if you merely want to use machine learning in a professional setting then master s degree should suffice you are still expected to understand and use university level math cuz let s be fair if you don t know what s a gradient or how to invert a matrix then you can t use any machine learning without it being black magic to you and have a reasonable background in statistics which again is generally taught at university it s one of the fields in which having a degree is by far the easiest way to get in as unlike some other specializations you do need theory and a good understanding of many computer science oriented topics from the get go and can t easily make up for it or learn as you go your python understanding is kinda irrelevant i mean sure it is being used in many machine learning frameworks and happens to offer many libraries that can be used for data normalization but these are nothing but tools which can be useful in solving a given problem in some cases you might have to write your own ones from scratch or extend existing ones immensely i mean in r machinelaerning you can find resources used to learn ml out of positions in that first comment either teach university level material or expect you to know it beforehand']

Name: 37_linguistics_nlp_computational linguistics_computational
Representative_Docs: ['have you looked at linguistlist dot org they post jobs aimed at linguistics majors all the time to be honest most of the jobs are in academia but there are occasionally some non academia jobs posted on the site otherwise as someone with an ma in linguistics too academia or comp ling are our only two options i would encourage your fianc e to consider taking an nlp course through coursera udemy or codeacademy they have classes about computational linguistics natural language processing and python would she consider teaching most ling jobs in teaching require phds but there are a few that will accept master s degrees poke around on thelinguistlist and see what you find good luck', 'if you have a master s degree in teaching english and have near native proficiency you might be able to get hired in certain countries are you interested in linguistics computational linguistics is becoming more of a popular field now that ai is taking off literary studies is tangentially related to linguistics so if you learned some of the core elements of comp linguistics there might be a pathway there', 'i m doing an undergrad degree ba in computational linguistics the previous answers hit the nail on the head our program is a healthy balance of linguistics fundamentals electives and cs although definitely not as comprehensive as a cs major we don t have courses about operating systems databases etc when we take nlp courses the linguistics background is nice to have but we are mostly just applying statistical methods to machine learning models lots of math and code how passionate you are about linguistics will affect which path you should take there are not many bachelor programs in computational linguistics in the us too so that might narrow your options']

Name: 38_engineer_team_product_meetings
Representative_Docs: ['i like to say i m a data scientist because i think that most accurately depicts what work i do but i often find people have trouble understanding what that work is i drop back to machine learning engineer if i see confusion or lack of understanding when i say data scientist my day to day workflow is fairly simple and often involves running and designing tests i work at a small startup with a core data science team developing nlp algorithms i test new features model architectures run validity reports for our models experiment with transfer learning and online learning and really mess about with anything else that goes on in our models i work almost exclusively in python although i recently expanded to javascript to help build out some infrastructure projects for the data science team honestly the workflow is phenomenal because everything that i do is test based if a test fails that isn t a reflection of my poor work but rather an insight that is equally valuable if a test succeeds then i work to implement the feature as quickly as possible i get to touch every layer of our software stack that has any data backend for infrastructure frontend for user data i often see my work go in week cycles for weeks i work testing a series of features and depending on the results of those tests i spend the next two weeks implementing further testing those features occasionally this cycle breaks when i have to do immediate analyses for clients because of the value of the deal size or when there is a long term engineering project that takes more than month to build most of the tweaks i make are quite simple learning rate tuning text feature ization preprocessing ect but occasionally we will rework the entire model devote resources to a new product which requires a significant amount of engineering resources i m incredibly lucky to have had this opportunity at such a young age and i could truly see myself doing this job for forever i feel like a kid in rd grade when the teacher says you can do anything for your science fair project want to see what happens when we ignore the last characters of words try it out i really am like a kid in a candy shop going nuts and luckily the proverbial candy i have chosen has had a significant impact on my company s success', 'data scientist although the work i do can be described as a hybrid between engineer analyst data scientist i m currently working long term on a new project involving text classification and ai i hate to sound buzzwordy but essentially we re building a system that classifies text and decides how to act on it it s not a chat bot but i guess it s similar about of my work revolves around this project and the rest is supporting data needs of my business function customer service self serve help center my day to day is pretty varied so i ll describe the functions i do in a typical week software engineering i m on a small data team that maintains our api service which takes in text and serves recommendations we do the software building and maintenance ourselves which involves anything from refactoring code adding features fixing bugs deploys configurations tuning parameters etc search and natural language problems improving our algorithms for search and natural language processing problems using python and elasticsearch this might mean tuning parameters writing or changing elasticsearch queries experimenting with new ways of analyzing text to improve our recommender system or working on ranking problems metrics analysis and reporting defining success metrics for projects and performance metrics for our apps services algorithms and setting up dashboards to monitor these things sql analytics software sometimes i also do ad hoc requests or deep dives to understand something i might answer questions like how are we doing at serving users in different languages or how is our partnership with certain third party service doing this usually involves making sure the proper data collection is in place the data is in a form we can analyze i might have to do some data modelling analyzing the data quantitatively and or qualitatively and summarizing and communicating my findings in a dashboard or report for stakeholders data infrastructure setting up etl tools and services in our projects designing running and analyzing experiments we have a solid experiment infrastructure at my company so luckily this number is a lot lower than it could be when our org wants to try out a new feature or understand how users interact with an existing feature how it could be improved i design an experiment usually an a b test help set it up usually splitting users into experiment test groups ensure the data collection is in place and build out a dashboard using sql some analytics software to analyze the results tableau or mode i might be involved in the decision making but usually i provide the dashboard to the stakeholder and discuss what the numbers indicate and they take it from there dataset building our project is young and requires a large dataset of labelled text we have people whose job is solely to label the text but sometimes i join in when i m blocked waiting for something i also ensure our dataset meets privacy standards by planning the method we collect redact and store our data meetings with stakeholders and people interested in using our data mentoring i m very junior myself but i ve been at my company longer than like of employees so i tend to help new team members get onboarded and figure out the company s tools services and where to go to ask questions about different things learning talks i get to spend time at work learning there are a lot of internal talks and i try to make it to at least a week i also make a point to meet with people outside my team and talk about what they re working on so i stay up to date on our company s tools and services coffee breaks more talking to coworkers but usually less about work probably other stuff too but this is how my last few weeks have looked', 'some of those positions you mentioned are completely different career tracks than software engineering while others vary depending on company i will try to give a general breakdown software engineering track software engineer writes code for a product software engineers mostly start out as generalists but can specialize some common specializations are devops ai ml nlp vision cognitive services and distributed systems software architect tech lead senior engineer generally someone who is more experienced in software engineering works mostly on system design and may not code day to day often responsible for the technical direction of engineers engineering manager manages engineering teams and generally does not contribute code but may often review code may be more of a people manager or product manager depending on company will often be responsible for a section of a larger product director manages managers generally responsible for an entire product front end backend infra etc other roles product manager works on determining product features and direction can both be technical or business oriented technical product managers may sometimes contribute code many technical product managers are former software engineers program manager may be the same as a product manager depends on company i e microsoft or works more on the business side such as leading a tech development program project manager this is generally more of an administrative role within a team works on planning tasks scheduling meetings tracking progress and deadlines are met this is not a usual move from engineering solutions architect works directly with customers to guide or implement software using the company s products i e helping customers migrate to cloud will not code for a specific product but may create demos and pocs also might briefly work on customer product a lot of engineers move to this position to reduce hands on coding and more customer interactions data analyst works to extract actionable insights from data may also work on cleaning data data scientist combination of software engineering and data analyst that builds pipelines and tools to manipulate data usually a heavier emphasis on math and stats than swe applied scientists usually someone with a higher degree generally a phd usually works on cutting edge research may not code day to day hope this helps']

Name: 39_gpu_cpu_gpus_cores
Representative_Docs: ['i can answer to the simultaneous calculations part i ve not worked for aaa games but i worked for hpc if you mean simultaneous literally then the answer is add as many processor cores as you need server computers are built differently from personal computers and some can have multiple cpu sockets each hosting multiple cpu cores the biggest single pcbs i ve seen had sockets and the most cpu cores one one socket i ve seen is this wasn t the same computer though more typically however people use cheaper servers with perhaps two or even single socket just like your pc and not so many cores perhaps or and then they would be organized into a larger array by some hyperviser system like xen or hyperv or kvm or esx which would expose the combined resources as virtual cpus which you can then distribute to a number of virtual machines running on such an aggregated server but this changed dramatically around when people started using gpus for machine learning gpu architecture is different from general purpose cpus and allows you a much higher degree of parallelism the number of cores is typically in the thousands on a single socket but they aren t as capable as general purpose cpus so essentially in terms of number of cores your g force video adapter may beat a small data center so say you build an array of such gpus google and facebook for example have specially designed gpus gpu arrays they use for workloads that require high degree of parallelism or these can easily go into millions of cores per workload', 'since you re a student i doubt you ll need a dedicated gpu for anything you do i was able to do most of my work using a crappy laptop the rest i did in the cs computer lab most schools if they do require specialized equipment will usually have equipment for loan or in a computer lab if they don t they are likely not a quality institution gt do i really need a dedicated gpu if i want to care mainly about programming no your cpu and ram is more important gt what programming fields do require gpu hpc high performance computing and game design are the big ones applications involving machine learning ai and operating systems may occasionally use gpus as well gt what s the minimum gpu i should be going for whatever is required to run your software tools ides vms etc and other software you would like to run since you re programming you can skimp a bit compared to a gaming pc most programs do not require extensive graphics', 'gt looking to get something decent on a budget for some people budget means for others it means and in both tiers you can find decent programming machines i suggest to be somewhat more specific p general guideline i follow is business class line up metal case as little plastic as possible a nice ips screen cores if possible bundled with gb ram i do like having more than be it out of the box or ability to upgrade it and an ssd with slim bezels big enough to work on small enough to pack in your backpack example notebooks you can look at that are not too expensive you can run ubuntu on each acer swift asus zenbooks dell vostro hp envy maaaybe lenovo thinkpad e but newer ones are meh specs you can expect out of them quad core cpu gb ram ability to run on batteries for hours generally full aluminium chassis at reasonable weight i use one around this level for my professional work and i find it fine if you have visibly more money dell xps hp spectre microsoft surface pro lenovo thinkpad t series apple macbook pro specs wise they are not all that different from ones above you primarily pay for better build quality generally superior screen and a thunderbolt port that is really useful if you want an external gpu that is of course you are looking for a portable notebook that you can do coding on it s not a desktop replacement that can run you machine learning training while simultaneously compiling linux kernel if you need that kind of a notebook then look at dell precision or some hp elitebooks warning though they are heavy']

Name: 40_project_fyp_final_final year

a journal paper although i didn t get it published i had taken one machine learning module during my degree and had a small amount of personal knowledge before i started i was aware of how deep learning worked and i had built some simple q learning agents for my machine learning module but that was the limit of my knowledge i probably spend the first months just reading around the topic and learning about different approaches and techniques planning my project learning the relevant technologies and examining exisiting work in the field took up the majority of the time i spent actually producing the code and the write up was only a fraction of the total work that being said i did choose to do a research oriented fyp and if i had instead chosen to produce a pure software project i would likely have spent less time on learning new techniques and more time on analysis design and code production is there a particular framework or requirement set for your fyp or are you free to produce pretty much anything you want']

Name: 42_students_class_teaching_teacher
Representative_Docs: ['hi all i am in a public health graduate school getting my master s i took a course that offered for the first time this year it s an introduction to health data science course my advisor and the course instructor himself states no prior knowledge regarding data science or coding is not required however that is anything but i struggle with this course daily it is an elective that takes so much focus away from other courses that are required for my major epidemiology only a few students who have experience in coding data science do well in this course the rest of us struggle my professor assigns homework assignments that requires us to write out codes that were not referenced or taught in class they are not even provided in the recommended textbook for this class he wants us to learn independently meaning we google for hours and hours on end how to do these assignments which are designed to only take an hour or two as he said moreover he continually cancels office hours and refuses to provide actual help with i confront him i think this is completely unfair i am paying this school to teach me not test me to see how good i am at googling sure sometimes you need extra help on assignments and have to google some stuff but this this is ridiculous almost nothing he says or presents in class is relevant to our assignments or projects i am extremely frustrated and moreover he has only graded two assignments and we only have a few days until the end of the semester this professor should not be teaching he is inept careless and so inadequate half of us paid to enroll in an online class to actually learn r the core topic of this class at first such an into online course was fine but then the actual class started moving at a really fast pace and he has skipped over several fundamental aspects like how to construct a loop i think he doesn t deserve the right to teach this class i think he deserves some sort of reprimandation for the way this course is designed it is way above beginner level and has caused me so much stress so can and should i talk about my experience with a dean or advisor what would you do in my position', 'i was on the other hand of the story and i can give you my point of you i studied at a billingual university where some class were split so the class can be tought in first language of the student in most of my class it was me and the other guy needless to say we were forced to work together since our schedule matched perfectly the problem was that i like to work fast and try to finish the work wa had to do prior to the night before due date and he was the complete opposite of that slow worker and last minutes i gave him the benefit of the doubt multiple time and everytime he sent me his work i reviewed what he did and he reviewed my work his work was either not working as ask in the project directive or he simply didn t follow directive this lead us to have poor grade and for someone like me it s not acceptable for someone else to ruin my academic career so we were a team for the first year fast forwarding to second year i asked my teacher if i could work alone and most of them agreed since they saw that our team wasn t working during that year he kept asking to see my code because his and his new partner wasn t working i m not stupid so i proposed them to help them debug their code instead and we worked like that for the whole year last year of our degree we had to do this year long project followed by a week exercise where our class were cancel so we could focus on that exercise since i needed a teamate and he was my only option we teamed up again this time i made a deal with him i would do all the weekly assignement and he would focus on the yearlong project every week we sat down shared our research note and our work in general two months before due date i found out we had nothing the research he did was just surface research and didn t understood or wasn t able to explain to me half of the term he was throwing my way for a little bit of context we we re working on a deeplearning neural network and we had knowledge on the subject prior to this project so i had to babysit him on a day to day basis work a ton on the project plus the weekly assignment and push him to do his work we ended up being late and asked for time extension and our final grade sucked but we passed our project didn t work because the tool we were using wasn t working for our project this is something that should have been discovered in the first months of the project not weeks before due date our theory was on point though and from my point of view it s because i doubled down on the project and push him and got mad at him yelling at him for things to move then comes the weeks exercise which was a class wide team person lol that we had each out own part to do which were all inter connected needless to say that the other people found out fast enough that we would need to do his work and carry him to the finish line all that being said i don t even know if he knew he got carried for all those years the moral is you might think your work was good or your effort was on par with every body on your team but you never know how much time every one puts on the project you say you re working a lot and even though you can t change that it can be frustrating for the others in your team that are actually working harder on the project than you that behing said acting like a moron about it and tossing your work without explanation and not letting you talk about your work in presentation is not acceptable i would talk with your team member about it be ready for critisism on that and try to find a comon ground on how to work this out if nothing happen i would ask your supervisor for advice', 'i m a high school science teacher with higher ed experience i have been an adjunct as a side gig for about years now and spent three years as a full time graduate assistant while working on my phd in teacher education at a research institution before returning to the classroom this year i am still finishing my degree and will be abd this spring to echo what most are saying the answers to all of your questions depends massively on where you land some key differences you will face across the board there is almost always a standardized test at the end of the semester year this means your content standards and often your curriculum is handed to you your experience with this test depends entirely on your admin if they are the type to get uptight about testing they will ride you on it if the admins are more invested in a holistic deep learning approach you will be able to stress less about the test the level of stress also depends on the stakes in my state biology is the science test for determining the school s effectiveness so biology teachers get a lot more pressure than teachers of other subject areas classroom management is a big deal in many schools in some schools the students mostly manage themselves you will be putting in way more than hours per week but the amount depends on your school as a teacher your enemy is time you always know in the back of your mind that you could be doing more but don t have the time energy many teachers face big issues with work life balance as others have said the work is exhausting until you gain your teacher legs and even then you re going to feel drained for roughly half of the year in terms of student apathy i find that the level of apathy is about the same for the high schoolers i teach as the undergrads masters students i have taught behavior management depends completely on your context parents are a much bigger factor in secondary ed phone calls parent meetings and emails will be an important part of your routine one of my undergraduate professors in my teacher ed program once told me that every teacher sucks at their job for the first years of their career this is part of why roughly of teachers quit the profession within years be prepared to feel this way of course you can t say every teacher but a huge percent do i want to re emphasize every school is different and your mileage will massively vary depending on where you are and what you are teaching your admins can make or break the experience for you with all of that said i chose to leave higher education to head back to high school i still do research and adjunct as side gigs however my passion is teaching science if you aren t passionate about teaching kids you will hate it for the most part teachers only teach for one reason the love of what they do before making any major decisions i would strongly recommend finding some teachers in your area and asking to shadow talk with them do this with more than one teacher in more than one school if you like what you see hear then go for it']

Name: 44_game_games_game development_video
Representative_Docs: ['the games industry usually hires people that specialize in a field so you will usually see job openings for graphics programmer or gameplay developer or core technology engineer etc that said it is advisable to try to be t shaped that is some surface area knowledge about a variety of fields but deep knowledge on the skill you actually want to specialize in so the first thing to be clear on is what do you want to specialize in that is the same as asking what interests you the most what gives you the most fun doing graphics ai audio physics networking gameplay are some examples of areas you can develop deep knowledge on as an example of being t shaped you can have deep expertise on graphics skills but it is not uncommon that graphics programmers also have knowledge about core tech skills like multithreading profiling memory and performance optimizations various gpu and cpu hardware architectures etc all of these skills are complementary to graphics and can be leveraged in other areas such as physics for example if you are not clear on what area you d like to specialize in just try different things grab a framework like sdl or sfml from github and run some samples to see what catches your eye play around with the code and change some variables or grab a game engine with a ready made example game or demo and do the same once you are clear on what you d like to specialize in make samples demos and games that showcase your skills in that area and create a portfolio where you post what you learn there one of the big issues is that you need experience to get into the industry but how can you get experience if no one will give you a job without experience it s a chicken and egg thing you break the cycle by learning on your own and finishing projects or going to a games programming masters that focuses on hands on projects that you can use on your portfolio that way you prove you have the experience and can get into an entry level junior position or internship at a good company to give a practical example let s say you d like to specialize in physics one path could be to explore some available physics sdks like bullet box d or physx and run some of their examples start with the simple ones and step through the code to see how it works change some variables to see the effect read the documentation read books like game physics engine development see what people on twitter that are physics programmers and work at games companies and lurk on their conversations with their peers follow their blog posts develop your own physics engine make a game with your physics engine do this consistently and market yourself join online communities post what you learn online so it is easily accessible that way you are showing your skills to future employers but also teaching others that might be on the same path as you once were trying to learn substitute physics for graphics or any other skill in this paragraph and the advice will still apply once you have this kind of deep knowledge it won t take you long to apply it to any of the game engines out there and most companies will even let you learn the engine on the job as for how important a degree is it is not that important to be honest you just have to show you have the skills now either you can do that on your own or maybe you need a roadmap and someone to push you a university degree gives you a roadmap and a structured timeline to finish your projects it also signals you can finish a project and endure i can t say i would recommend it to everyone because that depends on the quality of the degree and also the country you want to do it in a degree in the us is much more expensive than in other countries for example one way where a degree might be useful is if it gives you access to platforms and tools that you wouldn t otherwise be able to get on your own for example a degree that gives you access to write code for the playstation and you want to be a console programmer then it might be worth considering bottom line is you have to learn the skills apply them to finish projects and showcase them if you can do this yourself you might not need a degree', 'i think you should do way more research into this topic video game design is not programming animation isn t programming there isn t really video game programming sure there s computer graphics computer vision physics engine etc they aren t really video game programming but just general programming areas that are applicable to video games you won t be designing game mechanics or anything like that in those areas if you care at all about wlb money job security then the video game industry will be a gamble for you it s essentially an entirely different industry from the rest of tech swe crunch times are common and quite bad massive layoffs happen and are basically normal after every big project and the pay is definitely a notch or two or five lower than the rest of tech however you should definitely take electives you are interested in this is basically what college is for to explore the different areas and find your interest maybe you do have talent and passion for something related to the video game industry whether it be game design programming or animation work', 'unfortunately just liking video games isn t a good enough reason to work as an it professional i m telling you this out of my personal experiences as i when i started working in this field years ago my only real motivation was that i liked video games it took me years until i found a job where i liked what i do even a little bit because i realized that playing games is great but making software can be painfully boring development work itself requires a certain skill set that not many people posses or are willing to learn in my experience my jobs never required all that much math and i know a lot of people who have very successful careers that will claim programming doesn t require any math but that heavily depends on what you do and the level at which you do it i work in fintech and surprisingly i need to know next to zero math because we have quants that give us all the math related code we need to implement in easy to use libraries game development and ai on the other hand though will both require extensive mathematical knowledge especially in terms of statistic linear algebra and discrete mathematics also while we re on the subject of game development i d steer clear from it at any chance i d get game development blows in my region it s poorly paid and even in the us at large it s near the bottom of the pay scale and very career crippling you re stuck using ancient technologies like c or c unless you go into mobile gaming you ll be stuck in a development environment that s equally as antiquated where of the work you ll do is organized waterfall style and on top of that your job is constantly at risk because game companies go under far more frequently than other companies in this field my suggestion would be if you still want to pursue this career to focus on something that s modern and in demand right now and in the foreseeable future cloud technologies web development or ai forget game development do it as a hobby in your spare time if it makes you happy like most people over at r gamedev if you don t like math you can skip ai development as well furthermore if you re not very passionate about this field and don t feel like relearning a new language or tool every months i d steer clear of the flavor of the month programming languages like javascript or python as well as any ui related work tl dr math is not a requirement to be a good programmer but it helps game development is shit and you shouldn t do it']

Name: 50_ai_lawyer_ai research_ml
Representative_Docs: ['a career in ai there are a bunch of different career paths each with different minimum requirements i suggest you figure out which one you actually want to do and work backwards from that the two most obvious ones that come to mind are work as an engineer on ai product like writing code for waymo or working on worldwide systems like g translate or image identification work as the whole ml side of some marketing data analysis tool or something at a small startup company', 'there are a lot of career choices to make in software development and a lot of these tracks require Name: 57_programming_learn_new_programmers
Representative_Docs: ['you don t need to specialize yet your goal right now should be to write a lot of code and learn about a lot of different areas the vast majority of jobs are in some form of software development some of the largest areas are building web sites building apps and writing enterprise software stuff used internally at large businesses most programmers are working on relatively mundane things but it s still challenging work that pays well you hear a lot about machine learning and data analysis but there are still relatively few jobs for that and most people in those fields have a master s degree or ph d with a lot of advanced math experience i m not saying you shouldn t strive for that just realize that it s only a tiny fraction of programmers doing that stuff but there are lots of other important and interesting areas that need good programmers one of the best things about programming though is that you don t need an advanced degree or a lot of money in order to do just about anything anyone can write a game and publish it on the app store anyone can program a machine learning system to do facial recognition all it takes is time and effort the code is all out there there are free tutorials for just about everything you could imagine but again don t worry about it too soon your goal right now should be to learn there s almost nothing that would be a bad idea to learn', 'learning aws machine learning javascript frameworks these are worth playing with to see if you like them and maybe give you a direction to take your career in the future since you re a junior right now though that might be thinking too far ahead right now unless you are looking to escape java development your goal should be to just get that junior off your title and become the coveted mid level engineer who can hit the ground running and get things done right away because that s who people want to hire to that end unit automated testing writing tests is a big part of your job if not it should be and it will be in your next job and there is skill in writing good ones effective java by joshua bloch is a great book to read if you want to stick with java for any length of time since you re in a spring shop you have the opportunity to get familiar with spring itself and that can be useful understanding the guts of spring can help you design solutions and become more self sufficient as a developer in your current shop you can also look into alternative frameworks like dropwizard a spring boot competitor and other di frameworks like guice and dagger just to see some alternative design decisions sql a lot of java work is backend which means you ll be accessing relational databases and knowing how to write good sql and design relational data structures can help your code perform well and avoid errors i ve also used sql to help debug and make design decisions git helping your peers get out of git hell is a great way to look senior understanding how it works is part of your day to day git is popular enough that even if your current job doesn t use it your next job will kubernetes i just threw this in because everyone seems to love it', 'originally i pounded out codecademy treehouse and codeschool courses on html css javascript git bash and other basic stuff for a couple of months until it all felt familiar and comfortable i made a fun website on the side to apply my learning now that i m a fully employed software engineer i do most of my learning by either taking various courses i find and am interested in codecademy has good ones for python and some cs wes bos is great for es and react and i did a fun one from pirple for writing dependency free node apis and then challenging myself with side projects to learn by application i keep a list of fun ideas for things i want to build side projects are a great way to learn new languages and frameworks as well i ve found need an excuse to start learning java think of a fun side project and challenge yourself to use it in terms of learning things in order there s a lot of good advice around on various forums for the front end everyone says learn html css first and get really solid before getting more into the javascript and programming it s also important not to skimp on cs concepts like algorithms and data structures but mostly algorithms imho since your data structure knowledge will probably come as you gain experience with different languages and paradigms you may also need to learn specific technologies for a job focus on those and be aware of how broad or specific full stack or not your employer wants you to be be excellent at those technologies and learn others in your free time maybe find a mentor if you need more direction most importantly stay stoked stay in touch with what fascinates you about programming sometimes this means pushing through a dry textbook with basic stuff and other times it means watching talks on machine learning because it s awesome be a member of the community stack overflow reddit conferences work if you have it software engineering is both my job and my hobby i love it if you love it it will always be easy even when it s really hard books i like any well reviewed cs textbook eloquent javascript the c programming language the algorithm design handbook i really want to read clean code as well hope this helps a bit keep at it']

Name: 58_company_teams_team_work
Representative_Docs: ['gt is the common glowing perception of top tier tech simply inflated are there any other factors at play here else why would top tier tech engineers be so content to leave the supposed top tier behind and settle down in lower tier companies without looking back all comes down to what each individual values really top tier may be objectively true in the market share and reputation sense but it might not necessarily be true on an individual level some of my friends got jobs at faang companies right after they graduated from school it was the first company that offered them a job and it was a top tier so why not take it they left within a year for various reasons one realized she didn t like the feel of a big company and wanted something smaller so she left to join a fledgling startup and help build it another just wanted to move somewhere more exciting so he downgraded to a midsize startup that s doing pretty well another just wanted to get more hands on experience in another field ai outside of her team and moved into an opportunity at a small startup that would allow her to build bigger things in that field another just hated the politics of his team another got extremely lucrative salary offers from smaller companies the list goes on other people i know ended up getting jobs at a faang company and staying there for years without even considering moving companies they were happy with their teams their roles in the org their progression their great benefits their work life balance etc they had a lot of companies trying to poach them but whenever they were confronted with those situations they just didn t feel the need to move all depends on what you value and prioritize what kind of opportunities you re looking for and how good your team is', 'there s interesting work to be had in these companies but the simple truth is that joining a large company is a bit like joining the military no matter who you are or what you can do you re ultimately going to go where they need you and when you re the new guy you re always going to be sitting behind a large queue of people who better connected and quite possibly better period than you so you get the last pick of available teams and projects for example a good friend of mine who has a phd joined a research team and then spent his first two years working on testing frameworks he eventually got into some more interesting stuff but it didn t happen overnight i ve also spent a huge portion of my own career working full time in big whatever r amp d not just as an intern with a dog and pony project robotics iot stuff like that unless you re in a pure research lab like msr or ibm research i did intern with the latter but never worked there full time it s drastically different than the university environment where your primary goal is to publish papers and secure grant funding companies typically don t want to do this because they re funding your project in an attempt to gain a competitive advantage in the market this also means you won t have many opportunities to talk about your work you ll definitely sign a number of ndas typically unless you re pretty high up the chain director or vp level or equivalent e g principal sr principal distinguished engineer you re given some arbitrary but often fairly specific business requirement and then asked to solve it in a novel way best case or demonstrate the viability of a pre determined approach a much more common case at amazon for example it might involve removing the human element from package tracking in specific environments and process paths by the time it gets to you it s build a system that can track inbound and outbound pallets in a warehouse using rfid meanwhile somebody else is told to do it using computer vision and yet another group is tasked with investigating it with scanner gates or tunnels routing out of scope that s a different problem entirely from there you ll often try to build an mvp minimum viable product or prototype for demonstration purposes you often don t even have a choice of tech or stack and it s common to be discouraged from using non proprietary tooling stacks whatever if an in house alternative is available even at ibmr we were pretty much forced to run middleware services in websphere so in many ways it s actually much more restrictive than the production environment you might even be constrained to using a particular vendor with whom the company has already signed a contract again your job is to build the mvp good chance it fails either for technical reasons or because the business case or weak or for any number of other reasons and then you start over again on something else rarely will your work actually reach production at least within the timeframe you ll be involved with it all of this said there are serious perks to it it s still at least somewhat more interesting than the boring shit that the rest of the company is doing you are paid a lot of money deadlines and hours are often very relaxed you won t have on call rotations or other production concerns you ll usually be touching and working with some cool tech even if you have little control over what it is and you ll generally be working with some of the smartest people in the company but if you really value creative latitude don t expect big iron industry r amp d to satisfy it over the long term', 'i m actually a joint maths and cs student too so i m currently working as a software engineer in a tier investment bank last year i was at a startup though not really since the company was growing very quickly at that point had developers and employees total i m also in a top global ranking uni and hence have plenty of friends who are working at the big tech giants so i d like to think that i m rather well equipped to answer your question firstly i see from your comment that you want to avoid getting lost in a giant system of engineers and projects and management completely understandable and i totally feel the same way however what you may not realize is that a lot of the big tech companies actually have a lot of autonomy within their own teams after all in the tech field if you re slowed down by bureaucracy then someone else might overtake you many of my friends say they didn t feel at all like working in a large company with very little having to wait around for some business decision or approval process secondly there are a lot of common stereotypes about startups being scrappy or being unlimited fun where you can do whatever you want or being very stressful or whatever note that some of the things i ve said there contradict each other because it simply just depends on the company some places will have a culture of churning out as many new shiny bells and whistles as possible without caring much for engineering practices and tech debt whereas some have put far more care and dilligence into their codebase and architecture than the bank i m working at now there are however a couple of things that i do think are universal to a lot of startups the opportunity to learn and grow if you re at a big company chances are you ll probably not be starting any new products codebases and if you do you ll have to essentially do so from a pre approved template i e a java spring application for a rest api stack at a smaller company there s a higher possibility that you ll be able to make or influence decisions like that or have an idea about what could be done prioritized not only that but since tech roles in startups are usually significantly less set in stone you ll have to chance to learn new skills that you may not get to otherwise for example a front end dev has a much higher likelihood of being exposed to other areas of tech in the company like security data science analytics machine learning dev ops etc than at a place where there are dedicated hundreds of engineers in each team the opportunity to get recognised and advance your career the thing about big companies is that with large dev teams its harder to get recognized if youre doing well or poorly senior management won t know you re the one who was instrumental to that big cutting edge release just the name of the team that was behind it and if you re better than your coworkers you probably won t be recognized for it until you leave and they start missing you some of the stuff i have to deal with at my current place makes me want to fire whoever was responsible poorly designed database object oriented programming which is literally doing everything in a single multi thousand line class that said again it also does depend on the specific company team and culture at a smaller company either you get recognized for all the good work that you ve done and you are given advancement opportunities within the company maybe you go from being a junior to senior role pretty quickly if you re good you can put down all your achievements on your cv and get a higher pay more senior job at some other company what s more impressive on a cv being another cog in the wheel to just keep things running at a large tech company or building or making significant changes to a startup s tech architecture again it depends on the specific company and team some people do get pretty cool things to do at large companies though i d also argue they are given them rather than actively pursuing and leading the charge on initiatives like you would in a startup tech stacks at startups smaller companies are also generally newer and hence more valuable to your career tech giants are pretty good at keeping up but if you do go work in a large non tech giant then you ll probably be having to deal with a lot of legacy languages and technologies having modern hip and in demand tech under your belt graphql kubernetes react etc as opposed to c fortran will make you a lot more appealing work hours and expectations some startups are very fast paced meaning that you get to do a lot of shit but can also be a bit stressful and sometimes if you re the only one not going above and beyond normal hours then it does make you stand out a bit in a bad way that said it s up to you to decide for yourself whether thats worth the extra experience responsibility and potential advancement contrary to the above they can also be really chill unlimited holidays office parties and everyone knowing everyone sometimes it goes a bit too far imo and there s this expectation that you re a family but not always again up to your own discretion this also isn t exclusive to startups why do you think google facebook etc are trying to paint themselves as having startup culture large companies will be kind of the safe middle ground for this of course there s always the odd unreasonable and shitty manager karen every now and then see r talesfromtechsupport but thats not a big company exclusive thing and it s also easier to push back at unrealistic work expectations really i d say the best way to find out for yourself is to do internships and get some first hand perspective']

and lay that foundation secondly i d work on tackling larger programs you say you want to be able to solve complex problems i m interpreting that as write programs that are large enough i can t think of them in one piece learning to program with other people is vital but as it happens you in a month is basically the same thing you ll learn what makes your code hard to understand or unclear when you think to yourself what should i do first is a good place to be then try to break it up into smaller pieces until you reach a piece that you feel comfortable implementing', 'sooo i ve been lurking want my honest opinion then here s my take on this why would you confine yourself to only becoming a web developer why not keep it more general if your goal is to get a job at big companies chances Representative_Docs: ['yes involves more matrixes you can learn machine learning without linear algebra but to understand what goes on under the hood linear algebra is recommended', 'i don t think i could disagree with this more linear algebra is the big picture a machine learning algorithm is almost entirely linear algebra one cannot truly understand a ml algo without understanding linear algebra', 'linear algebra has to do with high dimensional spaces people here keep talking about matrices and vectors but that s the subset of linear algebra manipulation that is relevant to machine learning the subject is far more complicated than matrices and vectors that being said basics of linear algebra in the context of machine learning is important to understand what s going on there are likely mathematics dense ml books that deal solely with the stuff you care about rather than learning the entire subject of linear algebra also some basic calculus is also useful in terms of gradients and things but practical applications of this will still be heavily related to linear algebra more so than calculus']

Name: 66_neuroscience_brain_cognitive_psychology
Representative_Docs: ['apologies if i ever come off as rude as that s not my intention just trying to offer some perspective here first off i think it s great that you re intellectually curious about
